metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a concrete, bounded default for the conditional audit branch in COMPONENT 2: either (a) declare that fixing any subject-less emitter found is explicitly IN scope and cap it (e.g., 'fix at most the DET emitters in det_floor.py; file a follow-up for anything outside that file'), or (b) declare fixing out of scope for this ticket and note the audit is record-only, so AC3 can be evaluated without open-ended code changes.
findings.finding: COMPONENT 2 contains a conditional placeholder that defers a real implementation decision to executor investigation: 'If the audit finds a genuinely subject-less emitter, fix it; otherwise the backstop stands alone.' An executor cannot know which files to change, what the fix looks like, or whether the AC scope includes those changes until the audit runs — this is a 'depends on investigation' / 'check if' pattern that blocks scoping.
findings.location: COMPONENT 2 — DET finding content floor, paragraph beginning 'Backstop:' / AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'If the audit finds a genuinely subject-less emitter, fix it' with a bounded statement such as: 'The audit is record-only for this ticket; any subject-less emitter found is captured in a follow-up issue. AC3 is satisfied by the recorded audit result plus the backstop, regardless of audit outcome.' This removes the open-ended conditional fix from scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete artifact and proving command to AC3's audit sub-criterion — specify where the audit result is recorded (e.g., a comment block in det_floor.py, a docs/adr entry, or a test file fixture), and add a proving command such as `grep -r 'DET emitter audit' docs/` or `python -m pytest tests/test_det_emitter_audit.py` that asserts the record exists and names P5 as compliant.
findings.finding: AC3's 'audit of existing emitters is recorded' specifies no concrete artifact, location, or format — leaving the criterion effort-framed ('do the audit') rather than outcome-framed ('a named artifact exists at path X with content Y'). There is also no proving command that would verify the audit was performed and its conclusion documented.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC3 to: 'The audit result is recorded as a docstring or inline comment in det_floor.py (or a named doc file) naming each DET emitter and its compliance status; verified by `grep -n "DET emitter audit" src/rebar/plan_review/det_floor.py` returning at least one match.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (e.g., a kill-switch flag, expand-contract sequencing, or a dry-run/audit-log mode) for each of the three new drop-capable mechanisms so misfires are detectable and reversible.
findings.finding: The plan introduces three distinct drop-capable mechanisms (absence-claim veto in decide.py, DET hygiene backstop in det_floor.py, ac_unverifiable clearing in workflow_ops.py) that irreversibly suppress findings within a review run, but provides no rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing if any mechanism misfires. The 'byte-unchanged' and 'abstain-safe' language acknowledges forward compat but does not address how to recover or disable a malfunctioning veto/backstop.
findings.location: COMPONENT 1 (decide.py absence-claim veto), COMPONENT 2 (det_floor.py backstop), COMPONENT 3 (workflow_ops.py ac_unverifiable clearing)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single feature flag (e.g., `REBAR_ABSENCE_VETO_ENABLED`, `REBAR_DET_BACKSTOP_ENABLED`) defaulting to True; document that setting them to False restores prior behavior. Separately, emit a structured log entry each time a finding is dropped by these mechanisms so misfires are auditable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add or update an ADR (or doc section in the verification-contract reference) recording the absence-claim veto policy, the DET-tier hygiene backstop rule, and the updated verification contract shape (claims_absence, absence_confirmed_in_context fields).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new algorithmic contract — the absence-claim veto (two new verification binaries + a deterministic drop rule) — and modifies the shared verification contract shape used by both plan-review and code-review kernels. No new ADR or documentation update is mentioned to record this architectural decision for downstream contributors.
findings.location: COMPONENT 1 (verify.py contract binaries), Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Create ADR-00XX 'Absence-claim grounding veto and DET hygiene backstop' covering: the two new binary fields and their abstain semantics, the deterministic drop conditions, and the operator-attested suppression mechanism. Update ADR-0043 to note that the [operator-attested] tag now has a downstream DET-detection effect in workflow_ops.py.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a small calibration probe (e.g., run absence_confirmed_in_context against 10-20 historical findings with known-correct absence status) to validate the binary's accuracy before relying on it as a hard veto gate.
findings.finding: The absence_confirmed_in_context verifier binary relies on an LLM text-search judgment ('search the plan text provided; is there any provision matching the item claimed absent?') whose false-positive rate at scale (real absences incorrectly confirmed as present) is validated only by a single regression fixture. The plan does not include a measurement or probe to establish the accuracy of this lookup across a representative sample of prior findings.
findings.location: COMPONENT 1 — Pass 2 verifier prompt design; AC line 2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a new ADR (or update an existing one) to record the three novel architectural decisions introduced by this plan: the absence-claim veto in the shared verification contract, the DET emission/aggregation backstop scope, and the upstream severity_attributes mutation pattern — and include that ADR file in the Scope deliverables.
findings.finding: The plan introduces three novel architectural decisions — an absence-claim grounding veto in the shared verification contract, a DET-tier hygiene backstop at the emission/aggregation boundary, and upstream severity_attributes mutation to suppress ac_unverifiable before impact_plan — but the Scope section lists no new ADR or design-doc update to capture them. 'Design approved by operator 2026-07-12' is noted in the Why section but no written artifact is created or referenced as a deliverable.
findings.location: Scope section; COMPONENT 1/2/3 descriptions
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/NNNN-absence-claim-veto-and-attestation-suppression.md` capturing: (1) why the absence-claim veto belongs in decide.py (symmetric to cited-reference veto at :318-326) rather than in Pass-1; (2) why the DET backstop is applied at the emission/aggregation point rather than in pass3_over_findings; (3) why ac_unverifiable is cleared upstream of impact_plan (keeping the kernel byte-unchanged) rather than branching inside it. Add this file to the Scope list and reference it in the ACs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that each new drop path (absence-claim veto in decide.py and DET hygiene backstop in det_floor.py) emits a structured log entry (at minimum: finding index, drop reason, and the evidence that triggered the veto) so operators can audit and debug suppressed findings.
findings.finding: When the absence-claim veto drops a finding or the DET hygiene backstop drops a subject-less DET finding at runtime, the plan specifies no logging, metric, or trace to make these drops observable to operators. The existing cited-reference veto (decide.py:318-326) may already emit a log, but the plan neither confirms this nor extends equivalent observability to the two new drop paths.
findings.location: COMPONENT 1 — Pass 3 veto logic; COMPONENT 2 — DET backstop at det_floor.py emission/aggregation point
findings.suggested_fix: In the absence-claim veto block in decide.py, emit `logger.info('absence_claim_veto', finding_index=idx, claims_absence=True, absence_confirmed=False)`. In the DET backstop, emit `logger.warning('det_backstop_drop', finding_id=finding.id, reason='no_location_no_evidence')`. Add an AC line: 'Dropped findings from each new veto/backstop path emit a structured log entry with drop reason.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider whether C1 (absence-claim veto + regression fixture), C2 (DET hygiene backstop + emitter audit), and C3 (operator-attested detection + rubric vocabulary) should be tracked as separate child tickets, given each delivers independently testable value and carries a distinct reason to change; if kept together, add a brief rationale for the bundling to prevent future confusion.
findings.finding: The plan bundles three independently-releasable outcomes with three distinct reasons to change — (C1) a bug-fix to the false-absence verifier logic, (C2) a correctness backstop for contentless DET findings, and (C3) a new detection+suppression feature for operator-attested ACs — mixing heterogeneous change kinds (bug-fix + correctness rule + vocabulary/detection feature) in a single ticket. Each component has its own AC, its own test, and would deliver value if shipped alone.
findings.location: COMPONENT 1 / COMPONENT 2 / COMPONENT 3 sections; Acceptance Criteria

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one golden-example test that runs the new verifier prompt questions (claims_absence, absence_confirmed_in_context) against a representative plan text snippet and asserts the returned binary values match expected outputs, so the prompt→binary extraction path is exercised end-to-end beyond stored fixture replay (AC2).
findings.finding: The verifier prompt additions for COMPONENT 1 (claims_absence and absence_confirmed_in_context binary questions, added to the single-turn and agentic verifier prompts in verify.py and associated prompt files) are tested ONLY via regression fixture replay of STORED verdict data (the f1fa 11:44 verdict), which bypasses the actual LLM call. No AC specifies any test that exercises the new prompt questions against a live or meaningful stub LLM call to confirm the model correctly produces the expected binary values. Because these prompts run on every review by default (the path is newly defaulted), the suite can go green while the new questions elicit wrong answers from the LLM in production — the classic proxy-validation gap for a defaulted path.
findings.location: AC2; 'Pass 2: add two abstain-safe binaries…' (COMPONENT 1); src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py + verifier prompts
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC or sub-test under AC2: 'A prompt-level golden test feeds the f1fa plan excerpt (which contains the actor + artifact path the finding claimed absent) into the updated verifier prompt and asserts claims_absence=yes and absence_confirmed_in_context=no are returned; a second case feeds a truly absent item and asserts absence_confirmed_in_context=yes.' This can use a lightweight LLM stub that validates prompt structure and key phrase presence if live LLM calls are infeasible in CI, provided the stub is documented as faithful to the boundary (satisfying the proxy-validation anti-FP exemption).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `claims_absence`/`absence_confirmed_in_context` belong in the shared kernel `_BINARY_DESC` (applicable to code-review too) or should be plan-review-specific Binary fields — if the latter, model them in a `plan_review_verification_model`-scoped Binary extension analogous to how `PlanSeverityAttrs` extends `SeverityAttrs`, rather than in the shared `_BINARY_DESC`.
findings.finding: The two new binary fields `claims_absence` and `absence_confirmed_in_context` are placed in the shared kernel `_BINARY_DESC` / `_build_binary` (verify.py), meaning they appear in ALL gate models (plan_review, code_review, base). The plan justifies this as 'adding to the shared verification contract,' but the premise lookup ('search the plan TEXT provided') is plan-text-specific. The code-review gate has no plan text — its verifier context is a diff. Absence claims in code-review findings (e.g. 'the test for X is missing') have a different lookup surface than plan-text absence claims. The plan's own precedent for plan-review-specific severity axes is `plan_review_verification_model`'s `PlanSeverityAttrs` extension, not the shared `_BINARY_DESC`; the analogous pattern for plan-review-only Binary fields does not yet exist in the codebase.
findings.location: COMPONENT 1 / Scope: src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py (_BINARY_DESC / _build_binary)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the `code_review/workflow_ops.py` line-range reference in COMPONENT 3 from `:278-301` (which is `code_review_verify_inputs`) to the actual DET-enrichment site — `_det_enrich_verifications` at lines 357–380, called from `code_review_decide` at lines 408–415.
findings.finding: The plan asserts COMPONENT 3's `operator_attested` injection mirrors `code_review/workflow_ops.py:278-301` which 'DET-enriches churn90/hard_to_reverse_surface'. Reading those lines shows they contain `code_review_verify_inputs` (the Pass-2 setup function), not the DET-enrichment logic. The actual DET enrichment of `severity_attributes` with churn90/hard_to_reverse_surface is `_det_enrich_verifications` defined at line 357 and called at lines 408-415 of that file.
findings.location: COMPONENT 3 — operator-attested AC awareness, injection-point description: 'mirroring how `code_review/workflow_ops.py:278-301` DET-enriches churn90/hard_to_reverse_surface'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'mirroring how `code_review/workflow_ops.py:278-301` DET-enriches churn90/hard_to_reverse_surface' with 'mirroring how `code_review/workflow_ops.py:357-415` (`_det_enrich_verifications`, called from `code_review_decide`) DET-enriches churn90/hard_to_reverse_surface into `severity_attributes` before `pass3_over_findings` runs'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/test_review_kernel.py` (line 187 exact-set assertion) and `tests/unit/test_review_kernel_rules.py` (lines 76-78 exact-count assertion) to the Scope as consumers that must be updated when `claims_absence`/`absence_confirmed_in_context` are added to the Binary model.
findings.finding: The plan adds two new fields (`claims_absence`, `absence_confirmed_in_context`) to the shared `Binary` model in `verify.py`, but does not enumerate two existing tests that structurally pin the binary field set: `tests/unit/test_review_kernel.py:187` asserts `expected_binary == {*review_kernel.GRADED_BINARY, 'cited_reference_accurate'}` (an exact-set assertion) and `tests/unit/test_review_kernel_rules.py:76-78` asserts `len(fields) == len(review_kernel.GRADED_BINARY) + 1` (an exact-count assertion). Both will fail if the two new fields are added to the Binary model.
findings.location: COMPONENT 1 / Scope section; AC1 ('byte-compat test on the base model')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, after 'tests', add: 'update `test_review_kernel.py:187` (extend `expected_binary` set) and `test_review_kernel_rules.py:76-78` (adjust field-count assertion) for the two new Binary fields; or, if the new binaries are NOT added to `Binary` but kept as a separate dict, no update needed — clarify in the plan which structural location they occupy.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the `code_review/workflow_ops.py` line reference from `:278-301` to `:357` (the `_det_enrich_verifications` function) so the implementer finds the actual DET-enrichment injection pattern.
findings.finding: The plan cites `code_review/workflow_ops.py:278-301` as the reference pattern for DET-enrichment of `severity_attributes`. The actual `_det_enrich_verifications` function that performs this injection starts at line 357, not 278. Lines 278-301 contain `code_review_verify_inputs` and the DET-enrichment introductory comment block — not the implementation being referenced.
findings.location: COMPONENT 3 description: 'mirroring how code_review/workflow_ops.py:278-301 DET-enriches churn90/hard_to_reverse_surface'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'mirroring how `code_review/workflow_ops.py:278-301` DET-enriches' to 'mirroring how `code_review/workflow_ops.py:357` (`_det_enrich_verifications`) DET-enriches'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `plan_review_decide` accesses the plan text (or AC text) needed for `[operator-attested]` DET detection — either add `plan_text` / `ac_items` to `plan_review_decide_input`, or relocate the detection to a step that already holds the plan text (e.g. a new DET scan in `p1_readiness_shape` / `det_floor.py` that emits per-AC attested flags as a coverage field, then threads those through `det_advisory` into `plan_review_decide`).
findings.finding: Component 3's operator-attested DET detection is specified to happen inside `plan_review_decide` (workflow_ops.py:324), but `plan_review_decide` does not receive the plan text — its input is findings (Pass-1 output), verifications (Pass-2 output), and det_blocking/det_advisory lists. Detecting the `[operator-attested]` prefix requires scanning the original AC text in the plan, which is absent from this step's input contract. The code_review analog (churn90/hard_to_reverse_surface injection) reads from `diff_text` and finding `location`, both present in `code_review_decide_input` — but the plan-review analog has no equivalent plan-text field in `plan_review_decide_input`. As written, the mechanism cannot perform pure-DET detection at that injection point.
findings.location: COMPONENT 3 — operator-attested AC awareness / 'INJECTION POINT: it is injected … inside plan_review_decide (workflow_ops.py:324)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `_attested_ac_indices` helper in `det_floor.py` (alongside `_count_ac_items`) that scans the plan text for `[operator-attested]`-prefixed checklist items and emits their indices as a DET coverage field. Thread that coverage into `plan_review_precheck`'s output so `plan_review_decide` can receive it as an input field (no plan_text needed downstream), then do the per-finding lookup against those indices in `plan_review_decide`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the Component 1 veto spec whether `absence_confirmed_in_context='insufficient'` triggers the veto or abstains — recommend documenting it as abstain (same safe direction as missing answers), and confirm the deterministic condition is strictly `== 'no'` (not `!= 'yes'`).
findings.finding: Component 1's absence-claim veto logic does not specify the behavior when `absence_confirmed_in_context` returns `'insufficient'` (the standard third answer the binary vocabulary supports). The veto fires only on `claims_absence=yes AND absence_confirmed_in_context=no`. An `'insufficient'` answer means the verifier could not definitively find or rule out the provision (e.g. the plan text is long, or the provision is ambiguously worded). If `insufficient` is treated the same as `no` (i.e. the veto fires), valid findings are silently dropped when the verifier was merely unsure. If treated like `no-effect`, the veto correctly abstains — but this is unspecified. The plan says 'missing answers abstain' but does not state whether `'insufficient'` on `absence_confirmed_in_context` acts as abstain or as `no`.
findings.location: COMPONENT 1 / 'claims_absence == yes AND absence_confirmed_in_context == no -> dropped'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: 'The veto condition is strictly `absence_confirmed_in_context == "no"`; an `"insufficient"` answer on either new binary abstains (no veto, no drop), consistent with the abstain-safe default policy.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the unverified capability assertion for `absence_confirmed_in_context`: either cite an existing experiment/eval showing LLM verifiers reliably perform plan-text retrieval at the precision/recall the veto requires, or add a prompt design note (e.g. instructing the verifier to quote the matched provision) plus a targeted behavioral test covering both the single-turn and agentic verifier paths.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `absence_confirmed_in_context` as a Pass-2 LLM verifier sub-question that requires the verifier to perform semantic retrieval against plan text ('search the plan text provided; is there any provision matching the item claimed absent? quote it if found'). This is a new kind of LLM task — plan-text lookup/retrieval — distinct from the existing GRADED_BINARY sub-questions (which grade a finding's internal logic). It is the load-bearing fix for the motivating bug (a false-absence survived Pass-2 at validity 0.94). The plan cites no experiment, eval data, or prior benchmark establishing that a Pass-2 verifier (especially the single-turn, non-agentic variant, which has no search tools) can perform this retrieval at the precision/recall needed to make the veto reliable. This is an unverified capability assertion for the component that fixes the specific bug.
findings.location: COMPONENT 1 — Pass 2: 'absence_confirmed_in_context' sub-question definition; also 'the plan text provided; is there any provision matching the item claimed absent? quote it if found; yes/no/insufficient'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC or Scope section: a reference to an existing eval fixture (or a new one) that specifically tests the verifier's retrieval accuracy on the `absence_confirmed_in_context` sub-question, covering at least one true-presence case (provision present but paraphrased) and one true-absence case. The verifier prompt should also include an explicit instruction to quote the matched text, making the answer auditable and testable deterministically.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly that `claims_absence` and `absence_confirmed_in_context` join `_BINARY_NA_DEFAULT` (default `'na'`, excluded from validity when not applicable), consistent with the other condition-specific binaries (`committed_work_relies_on_unbacked_claim`, `respects_artifact_altitude`).
findings.finding: The two new binary fields (`claims_absence`, `absence_confirmed_in_context`) are added to the verification contract but their na-default membership is never specified. The existing contract distinguishes fields that default to `'na'` (abstain, excluded from validity) vs. `'insufficient'` (included, penalising) via `_BINARY_NA_DEFAULT`. Both new fields are condition-specific (they apply only when a finding asserts an absence), exactly like `committed_work_relies_on_unbacked_claim` which IS in `_BINARY_NA_DEFAULT`. Without specifying whether the new fields join `_BINARY_NA_DEFAULT`, an implementer cannot correctly build `_build_binary()` — an absent/non-applicable answer will either drag validity or won't (materially different behaviour).
findings.location: COMPONENT 1 — 'Pass 2: add two abstain-safe binaries' / src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py (_BINARY_NA_DEFAULT, _build_binary)
findings.suggested_fix: In the COMPONENT 1 spec, add: 'Both new fields join _BINARY_NA_DEFAULT (default "na") — they are condition-specific and must abstain (be excluded from validity) for findings that do not assert an absence claim, mirroring committed_work_relies_on_unbacked_claim.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the veto rule's behaviour for `absence_confirmed_in_context=insufficient`: either `insufficient` is treated as 'not confirmed absent' (finding survives, veto does not fire) or as 'abstain' (same effect) — and state this explicitly in the COMPONENT 1 Pass-3 rule.
findings.finding: `absence_confirmed_in_context` is a three-valued field (`yes/no/insufficient`) but the Pass-3 veto rule is specified only for `no` (`claims_absence=yes AND absence_confirmed_in_context=no → dropped`). The plan says 'Missing answers abstain (no effect)' but `insufficient` is an explicitly stated allowed value, not a missing answer. The processing protocol for `absence_confirmed_in_context=insufficient` is undefined: it could mean drop (conflating insufficient evidence with confirmed absence), keep (treating it like 'yes'), or abstain (treating it like a missing key) — three meaningfully different outcomes.
findings.location: COMPONENT 1 — Pass 3 veto rule / src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py (symmetric to cited-reference veto at lines 318-326)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Pass-3 rule: 'insufficient is treated as not-confirmed-absent (the veto does not fire) — only a definitive no from absence_confirmed_in_context triggers the drop.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify WHERE the raw AC checkbox text is available at the detection point, or move the `operator_attested` DET detection to a step that has access to the plan/ticket text (e.g., `plan_review_precheck`/`plan_review_verify_inputs` which call `assemble_context` and hold `pctx.plan_text`), and define how the flag is propagated to `plan_review_decide`'s per-finding severity_attributes.
findings.finding: The `operator_attested` DET detection is assigned to `plan_review_decide` (workflow_ops.py:324), but `plan_review_decide` does not have access to raw ticket AC checkbox text. Its inputs are `{findings, verifications, det_blocking, det_advisory}` (workflow_ops.py:331-334) — post-Pass-1 structured finding dicts, not the original plan or AC text. The 'exact case-insensitive prefix per ADR 0043' scan (`[operator-attested]` in AC text) requires reading the original ticket description/AC text, which is only available at earlier steps (e.g., `plan_review_precheck` which calls `assemble_context`, or `plan_review_verify_inputs` which reads `pctx.plan_text`).
findings.location: COMPONENT 3 — 'INJECTION POINT: injected into each finding's severity_attributes … inside plan_review_decide (workflow_ops.py:324)' / workflow_ops.py lines 324-386

findings.finding: The plan asserts a 'P6-class sibling-overlap detector' exists as a distinct DET emitter that omits subjects and needs fixing. A grep/read of det_floor.py finds no such detector: the only sibling/file-overlap emitter is the P5 interference branch inside `p5_task_dag` (det_floor.py:491-509), which the plan simultaneously declares already-compliant ('ALREADY emits subjects — it is NOT a target'). The motivating example from the Why section ('Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge — naming no tickets/files, unadjudicable') is the exact text of the P5 interference finding (line 499), meaning the plan's target emitter and its carve-out are the SAME detector. There is no separate 'P6-class sibling-overlap detector' to fix.
findings.location: COMPONENT 2 — DET finding content floor; plan text 'The fix targets the emitters that genuinely omit subjects despite computing them (the P6-class sibling-overlap detector and any peer the audit flags)'
findings.suggested_fix: Run the mandated DET-emitter audit first and name the concrete emitter(s) found non-compliant (by detector name + det_floor.py line range). If the audit finds none, scope COMPONENT 2 down to the backstop hygiene rule only and remove the 'P6-class sibling-overlap detector' fix claim.

findings.finding: The plan names 'the P6-class sibling-overlap detector' as the primary fix target for COMPONENT 2's subject-emission audit, but no such detector exists in the codebase. P6 in det_floor.py is the AC/DD quality checker (compound criteria, vague lexicon, verify-command lint); it has no sibling-overlap or file-interference logic. The sole sibling/file-overlap DET emitter is the P5 interference check (det_floor.py:491-509, `_file_interference`), which the plan explicitly excludes as 'NOT a target.' The audit directed at 'the P6-class sibling-overlap detector and any peer the audit flags' will find no such entity, leaving Component 2 without a concrete non-P5 fix target.
findings.location: COMPONENT 2 — DET finding content floor, plan text: 'the P6-class sibling-overlap detector and any peer the audit flags'
findings.suggested_fix: Since the P5 interference emitter already emits subjects, and P6 has no sibling-overlap logic, revise the plan to say: 'The audit targets all DET emitters other than P5-interference; if the audit finds no non-compliant emitters, only the hygiene backstop is implemented.' Remove the 'P6-class sibling-overlap detector' reference entirely, as it names no existing entity.

findings.finding: AC5's 'coaching remains non-prescriptive' is a subjective quality term with no concrete proving command — there is no deterministic test or structured artifact check that could produce binary evidence of non-prescriptiveness.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC5: 'coaching remains non-prescriptive (states the evidence expectation, not the implementation)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'verified by inspection: the new coaching move in MOVE_REGISTRY states the attestation evidence expectation (what evidence is needed) without naming a specific implementation file, function, or command the plan author must produce — confirmed by a reviewer checking the move text against the existing non-prescriptive moves in the registry'.

findings.finding: The `absence_confirmed_in_context` binary has a three-value vocabulary (`yes/no/insufficient`) but the Pass-3 veto rule only names the `no` branch (`claims_absence == yes AND absence_confirmed_in_context == no → dropped`). The plan states 'Missing answers abstain (no effect)' but that covers absent keys, not a present `insufficient` answer — the routing for `insufficient` is genuinely undefined, leaving a third-of-three enum value without a specified decision outcome.
findings.location: COMPONENT 1 — Pass-3 veto rule (decide.py:318-326 analogy) and the binary vocabulary definition ('yes/no/insufficient')
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to COMPONENT 1: 'insufficient (cannot determine) → abstain from veto (finding survives, same as a missing key); only an explicit no confirms the absence and fires the drop.' This mirrors the abstain-safe design of the existing plan-v2 axes.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
